title: A Worked Adversarial Loss Example
concept: Walking through one concrete mini-batch of discriminator scores to compute Loss_D and Loss_G by hand, showing how the very same score feeds two different loss values.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In one GAN training step, whose weights actually get updated?

options.text: Only the network whose loss is being computed that step
Whichever network scored higher that step

exercises.explain: Correct! Training alternates — one network's weights move per step.

cards.title: Let's actually run the numbers
cards.body: So far this has all been conceptual. Time to plug in real scores from a tiny batch and watch both loss functions spit out actual numbers — the same way it happens inside a real GAN every training step.

cards.title: Setting Up the Batch
cards.body: Suppose the discriminator scores a mini-batch of 2 real and 2 fake images with these real-probabilities: real images → p = 0.9, 0.7; fake images → p = 0.4, 0.2. We'll compute Loss_D from all four scores, and Loss_G from just the two fake scores.

cards.title: A talent-show scorecard
cards.body: Picture a talent show with 2 real acts and 2 lookalike impersonators. Judges (the discriminator) give each act a "genuine" score. The show's producer (Loss_D) grades the judges on how well those scores matched reality. The impersonators' coach (Loss_G) grades the impersonators purely on how high their scores climbed, regardless of what's true.

cards.title: Computing Loss_D and Loss_G
cards.body: Loss_D averages BCE across all 4 scores: real (target 1) → -log(0.9)=0.11 and -log(0.7)=0.36; fake (target 0) → -log(1-0.4)=0.51 and -log(1-0.2)=0.22. Average ≈ 0.3. Loss_G only uses the 2 fake scores, wanting them pushed toward 1: -log(0.4)=0.92 and -log(0.2)=1.61. Average ≈ 1.27.

cards.title: Same fake scores, different math
cards.body: You might think both networks compute the same loss from the same fake-image scores, since it's the same 4 numbers. In reality, the discriminator's math treats p=0.4 as "pretty good, target is 0, low-ish loss," while the generator's math treats that exact same p=0.4 as "not fooled enough, target is 1, higher loss." Same input number, opposite grading direction.

cards.title: Loss_D vs. Loss_G on the Same Fakes
cards.body: How the two losses treat p = 0.4 and p = 0.2 differently:

left.text:
Target = 0 for fakes
Formula: -log(1-p)
Lower p = lower loss

right.text:
Target = 1 for fakes
Formula: -log(p)
Higher p = lower loss

exercises.prompt: In the worked example, the discriminator scores a fake image at p = 0.2 — barely convinced. Which statement is true?

options.text: It contributes a small loss to Loss_D (target 0, good catch) but a large loss to Loss_G (target 1, bad fooling)
It contributes zero loss to both networks
It contributes a large loss to Loss_D and a small loss to Loss_G
It only affects Loss_D, since Loss_G ignores fake scores entirely

exercises.prompt: True or false: Loss_G is computed using the real image scores in the batch.
exercises.explain: False. Loss_G only uses the fake-image scores — the generator never touches real images.

exercises.sentence: For a fake image, Loss_D uses target 0 while Loss_G uses target ___ for that very same score.
exercises.explain: Same score, opposite target: the discriminator wants to catch it (target 0), the generator wants it to pass (target 1).

exercises.prompt: Given a fake image scored p = 0.4 by the discriminator, which pair of loss contributions is correct (rounded)?

options.text: Both contributions are exactly 0.40, matching the raw score

exercises.prompt: A new fake image gets discriminator score p = 0.6. Which of these correctly computes its contribution to each loss (rounded to 2 decimals)?

options.text: Loss_D uses target 0: -log(1-0.6) = -log(0.4) ≈ 0.92. Loss_G uses target 1: -log(0.6) ≈ 0.51
Both losses equal 0.60, since that's the raw score
Neither loss can be computed without a matching real image for comparison

exercises.explain: Exactly — same score, opposite grading direction, computed from formulas you now know cold. Next: let's step back and see just how unusual this entire two-loss setup really is.
